sentences.text: My name is Lyonell Fliss.
L-Y-O-N-E-L-L F-L-I-S-S.
This was my name at birth.
Well, my parents and my friends gave me nicknames, like Nelu.
Nelu is one of the most common names in Romania, as nicknames.
It's a shortening of Lyonell.
It's N-E-L-U.
But for my parents and for my closest friends, I was Nelutzu, N-E-L-U-T-Z-U, which means little Nelu.
My birthdate was 1st of April, 1935.
My age now is 61.
I'll turn in April 62.
The city was Iasi.
It is spelled in the Romanian I-S-- I-A-S-I, with a certain accent.
In French or German, it's called Jassy, J-A-S-S-Y.
The country, Romania.
I can.
Iasi was called the sweet town of Iasi, because it was a dormant town, an old town, a cultural town.
It was the center of Romanian culture, where the roots of the Romanian culture took place.
Iasi is the provincial capital of Moldavia, one of the three provinces which form Romania today.
It is located on the hills of Moldavia, close to the Carpathian mountains, in an area which is famous for its wines and vineyards.
Actually, my first memories go to the farm of one of my uncles.
Actually, he was an uncle of my father.
He had a vineyard, and every Sunday the whole family was traveling from Iasi to the vineyard in a cart with two horses called dokar, and we are spending there a wonderful time.
I can remember that my father had a shop.
He was a shopkeeper.
Fabrics, he was selling, particularly to the peasants of the area.
And I was playing in the shop, as a little kid, sometimes upsetting my father's business.
Early memories go to strolls through the town to the-- to famous gardens, public gardens, in this town, where great poets of Romania were composing their poems.
I still can visualize the house where I was born and we lived for about three or four years, and from where we moved into a different house on the main street of Iasi, which was quite an improvement.
On Strada Bratianu, B-R-A-T-I-A-N-U.
Not far from my father's shop, just, let's say, ten minutes' walk.
I do remember the marketplace, an open marketplace.
I do remember the hospital just across the road from my father's shop.
And actually, the house where the shop was accommodated the rest of our family upstairs, where my grandfather, my grandmother, were living there, and together with them some of my aunts and uncles.
It was a big house.
I do remember that it was a very big dining room, where the whole family was reunited on Shabbat evening, and with occasions of other holidays or birthdays.
In this time-- so I'm talking about the period before the Second World War-- a lot of people were spending their summer holiday at various spas which were not far from Iasi.

The whole area was so beautiful and so green, and the fresh air was everywhere, that at distances of about 50, 60, 80 kilometers around Iasi, there were famous spas with good food, good rest, and good mineral waters.
And the rule was that the grandparents, they were taking the grandchildren there for the sole purpose to get them strong and fed, and feed them up to they couldn't eat anymore.
And the greatest reward was when coming back after a month to put them on a scale, and to find out how much weight they gained.
Exactly the opposite of our days.
I was actually, in this respect, a failure, to the disappointment of my grandmothers.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were happy days, because the whole family was united, and from my mother's side and from my father's side, the grandmothers and grandchildren and the daughters and the sons, they were going there and spending a wonderful time.
Yes.
My grandparents from my mother's side, they were from Blitzstein family, B-L-I-T-Z-S-T-E-I-N.
My grandfather was a dealer in chinaware.
He had a very large wholesale store in the city of Galatzi, G-A-L-A-T-Z-I, on the Danube River shore.
It was a very big operation, and they were quite well off.
He married my grandmother last century sometime-- can't give the exact date-- and they had six children.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was the eldest of all.
My grandfather passed away a year after I was born, so I didn't know him at all, but only from photographs and stories.
My grandmother lived longer, and she died in Bucharest, in the '50s.
Yes, both families, actually.
From a family tree which my father drew to me, and my mother also from her side, they show that they were established there since the 18th century.
From my father's side, they were living in the town of Vaslui, V-A-S-L-U-I.
From my mother's side, from Galatzi, as I said.
The story with the family of my father, my grandfather, [?
Leba ?] Fliss, married also last century.
Betty was his wife's name, my grandmother.
They were quite religious, and my grandfather actually was one of the earlier Zionists, but an unsuccessful one.
He tried three times to settle in Israel-- actually which was Palestine in those times-- and harder he tried, he never managed to establish himself, and came back.
And the last time when he came back, he actually came during the First World War.
He was taken to the army, he fought against the Germans in the First World War, got sick of typhus, and came at home very sick, almost dying.
And when he saw that he would be a burden for his family, he took his life by shooting himself with the same weapon that was given to him for the front line.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My grandmother then had to sustain the whole family of six children.
Well, my schooling started during the war, so there were a lot of events which happened in this period of time in Romania, and actually in the whole Europe, in those times.
My schooling time started in 1941, when I was six years and three months old, four months old.
In those times, the Jews of course went through a very traumatic time a year before, and their lives were in great danger.
All kind of laws and regulations were issued, and the Jewish children were not allowed, in the whole country of Romania, to attend public schools.
They were banned from the public schools.
I do remember, while passing with my mother in front of a public school, and ask her when I'm going to attend the school.
And she said, unfortunately, you're Jewish.
You're not allowed to attend this school.
So the Jewish community in Iasi-- I have to say that it was almost 50% of the whole population of this town was Jewish, like many other towns in Moldavia.
The Jews were living generally in towns, and the Christians either in towns but mostly on the countryside.
So the Jewish community organized the schooling of their children.

So I attended the first three years of my schooling in a makeshift school in a synagogue, where the synagogue hall was divided in four squares without any partitions in between, and there were four classes, like standard one, two, three, four, in the same hall.
It was of course a little bit difficult when four teachers were teaching simultaneously, but this were the conditions where we learned the first years of schooling.
Were you aware of anti-Semitism?
Yes, very much so.
Actually, Iasi was called the capital of anti-Semitism of Romania, where the Iron Guard had its strongest establishment.
And the members of the Iron Guard, they were wearing green shirts to be distinguished from others, and they were very proud of this, and big fur hats like shepherds.
And the way they were treating the Jews, I wasn't aware, because I was always protected by my parents.

But I still heard of beatings of Jewish students, before they were banned from the Iasi University, one of the oldest university-- if not the oldest university-- of Romania, with a long cultural tradition.
And the Jewish students were beaten to pulp by this Iron Guard legionnaire called [?
Der ?] [?
Koligs. ?] This was the cultural center of Iasi.
I didn't of this sort.
I wasn't-- I was too small, and when I grew a little bit up, there were no Jewish students anymore at Iasi University.
They were thrown out just before the Second World War.
My grandmother from my father's side was very religious.
From my mother's side, they were, I would say, less.
But the generation of my parents, they were non-religious.
They were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] celebrating, keeping the big-- the high holy days, Pesach, Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, the Shabbat lighting candles, but this was about all.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the death trains were related to the totality of events, to the pogrom, which took place in Iasi, so cannot be separated.
So if it's going to discuss the death trains, we have to discuss the whole event of the pogrom of Iasi.
And let's talk about this subject, that I think is the focal point of my memories.

[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In the month of June 1941, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when Romania is in full summer, it's very hot, it's pleasant weather, in good times-- what my father used to call normal times-- people go to spas, holidays.
The German army invaded Romania.
I cannot call a proper invasion, because Romania became an ally of Germany.
It is disputable.
Some people say that Romania was forced to become an ally, but in any case, with the approval of the government, a very large German army came into Romania.
Some of them, probably, from the occupied Poland, which was conquered in 1938.
So we're talking three years after this event.
So the German army was at its peak.

They were preparing themselves with the Romanian army, and you could have felt in this town movement of large numbers of German soldiers and Romanian soldiers, like preparing for something that is going to happen.
I was a child, so I saw soldiers, they looked great to me.
Soldiers, particularly the German soldiers, very well-dressed and neat.
Romanian soldiers.
And-- but I knew that my parents were very worried about all this.
And all of a sudden, on the 29th of June, 1941, in the morning, we had shootings in the street.
We were living on the central street, on the second-- on the first floor of a block of flats.
There were shoutings and people crying and rushing and running here in the street.
And my mother said, there is a pogrom.
I didn't know what the pogrom is, but this was what she reckoned that is going to be.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first measure she took, she locked the front door of the flat.
We were living in a two bedroom flat with a little lounge.
And the three of us-- my father, my mother, myself-- we went under the bed to hide ourselves.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It didn't pass longer than half an hour, and strong blows on the front door came.
But the door was strong, it didn't break.
And all of a sudden, we heard blows like an ax, and the door was opened then.
The three of us were in hiding under the bed with a bedcloth above.
A man rushed in, and he was Enache, E-N-A-C-H-E, the caretaker of the block of flats.
He was the one with the ax, who broke the door.
And swearing around, [SPEAKING ROMANIAN], it's where this bloody Jews run away.
Because he couldn't see us.
But he want probably just to kill us with his ax and loot the flat.
And he was very upset that he couldn't find us to split our skulls with his ax.
But he was just a petty criminal.
He was quite happy to take the cover sheet off the bed, to fill it with clothes and with the silver cutlery and everything what he found there, and he was quite happy with this.
And took all his loot and disappeared, leaving the door open.
